steps.prompt: Which word is the main finite verb?

hints.intrare: 'Intrare' is an infinitive, not a finite verb — it depends on 'Contendite'
hints.angustam: 'Angustam' is an adjective meaning 'narrow' in the accusative
hints.portam: 'Portam' is a noun meaning 'gate' in the accusative

hints.to be content: 'To be content' is 'contentum esse' — 'contendere' means 'to strive'

hints.indicative: Present indicative 2nd plural would be 'contenditis' — 'Contendite' lacks the -s
hints.subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'contendatis' — 'Contendite' is a direct command
hints.infinitive: The infinitive would be 'contendere' — 'Contendite' is the plural imperative

hints.contineo, continere, continui, contentum: These are the principal parts of 'continere' (to contain/hold)
hints.contemno, contemnere, contempsi, contemp: These are the principal parts of 'contemnere' (to despise)
hints.confero, conferre, contuli, collatum: These are the principal parts of 'conferre' (to bring together)

hints.imperative: The imperative would be 'intra' (sg.) or 'intrate' (pl.) — 'intrare' ends in -re
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'intret' or 'intrem' — 'intrare' is infinitive
hints.perfect infinitive: Perfect infinitive would be 'intravisse' — 'intrare' is present

hints.nominative: Nominative feminine would be 'angusta' — the -am ending marks accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative feminine would be 'angusta' — 'angustam' is accusative
hints.genitive: Genitive feminine would be 'angustae' — 'angustam' is accusative

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'porta' — the -am ending marks accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'porta' — 'portam' with -am is accusative
hints.dative: Dative would be 'portae' — 'portam' is accusative after 'per'

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'portae' ends in -ae
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'portae' ends in -ae
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'portae' ends in -ae

steps.prompt: What kind of construction is 'per angustam portam'?

hints.prepositional phrase with ablative: 'Per' always governs the accusative, never the ablative
hints.ablative absolute: An ablative absolute requires a noun and participle in the ablative — this has 'per' + accusative
hints.accusative of extent: Accusative of extent has no preposition — 'per' governs the accusative here

translation: Strive to enter through the narrow gate
